title: Driveline Care
subtitle: Keeping Your Exit Site Safe
description: Step-by-step instructions for daily driveline inspection and dressing changes.

sections.title: Why Driveline Care Matters
sections.content: The driveline exit site is the most common source of infection in LVAD patients. Proper daily care significantly reduces this risk and protects your health.

sections.title: Daily Inspection Checklist
items: Check exit site for redness, swelling, or warmth
Look for drainage — note color, amount, and smell
Ensure driveline is secured and not pulling
Verify dressing is clean, dry, and intact
Check for any new pain at the exit site

steps.title: Gather Supplies
steps.content: Collect: sterile gloves, chlorhexidine swabs, sterile gauze (2x2), split gauze, medical tape, and a clean surface.

steps.title: Wash Your Hands
steps.content: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water for at least 20 seconds, or use alcohol-based hand sanitizer.

steps.title: Remove Old Dressing
steps.content: Carefully peel away old dressing starting from the edges. Never pull on the driveline itself.

steps.title: Inspect the Site
steps.content: Examine exit site carefully. Note any changes from prior dressing change. Take a photo if you see something unusual.

steps.title: Clean the Site
steps.content: Using chlorhexidine swab, clean in a circular motion starting at the exit site and moving outward. Allow to dry completely (30 seconds).

steps.title: Apply New Dressing
steps.content: Place split gauze under and around the driveline. Cover with a 2x2 sterile gauze pad. Secure firmly with medical tape.

steps.title: Secure the Driveline
steps.content: Ensure driveline is anchored to prevent pulling. Use the stabilization device as shown by your VAD coordinator.

sections.title: Signs of Infection — Call Your Coordinator
items: Increasing redness or redness spreading beyond the exit site
Increasing pain or tenderness at site
Site feels warm to the touch
Swelling or hardening under the skin along driveline path

quiz.question: How often should you inspect your driveline exit site?
quiz.explanation: Daily inspection of the driveline exit site is essential to catch early signs of infection or problems.

quiz.question: What should you do if you notice green drainage from the exit site?
options: Apply extra tape and monitor for a week
Wait to see if it resolves on its own
quiz.explanation: Green, yellow, or foul-smelling drainage is a sign of infection. Always contact your VAD coordinator immediately.

quiz.question: When cleaning the exit site, you should clean from the outside moving inward.
quiz.explanation: False. You should clean starting AT the exit site and move outward in a circular motion — this prevents introducing bacteria toward the wound.

quiz.question: What temperature indicates a fever you should report?
quiz.explanation: A temperature of 100.4°F (38°C) or above is considered a fever and should be reported to your VAD team.
